zh.lifeSummary: Hesceline Jeanne De Haran De Bignon是Gauthier de Haran de Bignon和N de Villebon的女兒，於1110年出生在法國崔-瓦爾 德 洛易洛易特勒 比農-米拉波奧。她和Seigneur Josselin de Beaumont en Gâtinais II至少有2個兒子。她去世在法國瑟-吉馬內豐泰布利奧。

nameMeanings.name: De
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Haran
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Fidelma.
Irish: variant of   Haren  .
Irish: in central Ireland a shortened form, via Ó hArracháin, of Hanrahan .

variants.name: Haren
meanings: Irish (Clare and Connacht): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEaghráin ‘descendant of Eaghrán’, a diminutive of Eaghra, a personal name of unexplained etymology, as in O'Hara .
Irish (Fermanagh): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hAráin ‘descendant of Arán’, a personal name of unexplained etymology.
Irish (Armagh): variant of   Herron  .

variants.name: Hanrahan
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Kieran, Brendan, Ciaran, Connor, Liam, Seamus, Sinead.

Irish (North Munster and Kilkenny): shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hAnradháin ‘descendant of Anradhán’, a personal name from a diminutive of ánrad(h) ‘hero, warrior, champion’, a title denoting the nobleman next in rank to the king in medieval Ireland. The title was also used to denote court poets of the second rank. Later pronunciation in central Ireland as Ó hArracháin or Ó hArragáin, it has resulted in alternative Anglicized forms Haran , Harrigan , and Horgan .

nameMeanings.name: Jeanne
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Feminine form of   Jean .

variants.name: Jane
meanings: Originally a feminine form of John , from the Old French form Je(h)anne. Since the 17th century it has proved the most popular of the feminine forms of John, ahead of Joan and Jean . It now also commonly occurs as the second element in combinations such as Sarah-Jane. In Britain it is still one of the most frequent of all girls' names. It is not a royal name, but was borne by the tragic Lady Jane Grey ( 1537–54 ), who was unwillingly proclaimed queen in 1553 , deposed nine days later, and executed the following year. Seventy years earlier, the name had come into prominence as that of Jane Shore , mistress of King Edward IV and subsequently of Thomas Grey , 1st Marquess of Dorset, Lady Jane's grandfather. Jane Shore's tribulations in 1483 at the hands of Richard III , Edward's brother and successor, became the subject of popular ballads and plays, which may well have increased the currency of the name in the 16th century. A 19th-century influence was its use as the name of the central character in Charlotte Brontë's novel Jane Eyre ( 1847 ). From 1932 to 1959 it was used as the name of a cheerful and scantily clad beauty whose adventures were chronicled in a strip cartoon in the Daily Mirror. It is also borne by the American film stars Jane Russell ( 1921–2011 ) and Jane Fonda ( b. 1937 ).

en.lifeSummary: Hesceline Jeanne De Haran De Bignon was born in 1110, in Le Bignon-Mirabeau, Loiret, Centre-Val de Loire, France as the daughter of Gauthier de Haran de Bignon and N de Villebon. She had at least 2 sons with Seigneur Josselin de Beaumont en Gâtinais II. She died in Fontainebleau, Seine-et-Marne, France.
